merged_events.event_description: First U.S. consulate opened in Württemberg (Stuttgart, the capital).

evidence.quote: The first U.S. consulate opened in Württemberg on October 13, 1842, and closed on March 3, 1843.

merged_events.event_description: The first U.S. consulate in Württemberg closed.

evidence.quote: The first U.S. consulate opened in Württemberg on October 13, 1842, and closed on March 3, 1843.

merged_events.event_description: U.S. consulate reopened in Stuttgart (the capital of Württemberg).

evidence.quote: The U.S. later opened a consulate in Stuttgart on March 3, 1847, which closed in September 1985.

merged_events.event_description: George Bancroft was specially accredited as U.S. Minister to the Kingdom of Württemberg (nonresident, as he was also accredited to Prussia and the North German Confederation), signing a naturalization treaty on that date.

evidence.quote: On July 27, 1868, specially accredited U.S. Minister to the Kingdom of Württemberg George Bancroft signed a naturalization treaty with the Kingdom of Württemberg. At the time, Bancroft was also accredited to Prussia and the North German Confederation and, after 1871, to the German Empire.

merged_events.event_description: Württemberg joined the German Empire; foreign policy thereafter conducted through Berlin, ending separate U.S. diplomatic representation to Württemberg.

evidence.quote: The Kingdom of Württemberg was one of the founding states of the German Empire, which was proclaimed on January 18, 1871.

merged_events.event_description: U.S. severed all diplomatic relations with the German Empire, including Württemberg as a constituent state.

evidence.quote: On February 3, 1917, U.S. President Woodrow Wilson instructed the Secretary of State to notify the German Ambassador to the United States that all diplomatic relations between the U.S. and the German Empire were severed. As the foreign affairs of Imperial Germany were run out of Berlin and decided upon by the Kaiser, this constituted the severance of relations with Württemberg, as part of the German Empire.

matched.notes: Exact match on date and status. First U.S. consulate opened in Stuttgart.
Exact match on date and status. First consulate closed.
Exact match on date and status. Consulate reopened in Stuttgart.
Exact match on date and status. George Bancroft specially accredited as nonresident Minister to Württemberg (also accredited to Prussia and North German Confederation).
Exact match on date and status. U.S. severed diplomatic relations with the German Empire, which included Württemberg.

missing_from_csv.event_description: Württemberg joined the German Empire on January 18, 1871. Foreign policy was thereafter conducted through Berlin, effectively ending separate U.S. diplomatic representation to Württemberg.
missing_from_csv.notes: Medium confidence event. The RDCR indicates that after 1871 Württemberg's foreign affairs were handled through Berlin. This could represent the de facto end of the nonresident envoy relationship (transition from Envoy Nonresident to None), with the 1917 event being the formal severance of relations with the German Empire as a whole. The CSV currently jumps from Envoy Nonresident (1868) directly to None (1917). Consider whether an intermediate None status in 1871 is warranted, which would mean the 1917 event might need reinterpretation or the consulate in Stuttgart (which remained open until 1985 per RDCR) maintained the relationship. The situation is nuanced — the consulate continued operating, so the status may not have been 'None' in 1871.
